era: 1933. Three years after the Miskatonic University Expedition. Dr. William Dyer published his account; nobody believed him. The university quietly distanced itself. The Starkweather-Moore expedition is the second attempt.

geography.McMurdo Sound: The base of operations. Ships, supply caches, the last connection to the world.
geography.Ross Ice Barrier: The wall of ice marking the edge of the navigable south. Two hundred feet of vertical ice.
geography.Beardmore Glacier: The largest valley glacier in the world. The crossing point from coast to interior.
geography.The Plateau: The high Antarctic interior. Miles of ice over dead rock. 8,500+ feet above sea level.
geography.The Mountains: Lovecraft's unnamed range. Peaks exceeding 35,000 feet (higher than Everest). Black, bare of snow. Windswept. Caves with regular openings. Formations that look like cubes clinging to peaks.
geography.The City Beyond: Ancient ruins behind the mountain range. Cyclopean architecture. Murals that tell the history of Earth's true first civilization. The destination the players don't know they're heading toward.
geography.The Tunnels: Fifteen-foot carved passages descending from the city into warmth. Crude carvings overlay the originals. Giant blind albino penguins. An underground ecosystem. The temperature is wrong.
geography.The Violet Mountains: A dim, elfin line of pinnacled violet on the western horizon. The Elder Things feared them. Cities built there crumbled before their time. Something lives there that the Elder Things refused to carve clearly.

environmentRules: Wind is not just weather — cadences sometimes hold vague suggestions of wild and half-sentient musical piping. The wind has a voice.
Mirages are vivid and persistent. Distant mountains float in the sky as enchanted cities. Reality is unreliable from the start.
Light is always wrong. The midnight sun. Red-gold light on black peaks. Opalescent voids where sky and snow merge.
Cold is actively hostile. Equipment fails. Fuel freezes. Exposed skin dies.
Silence is the most terrifying sound in the Antarctic. The absence of sound is a narrative signal.
Every scene should include sensory details of the environment. This is not a comfortable adventure.
Equipment malfunctions near Elder Thing technology — electrical and magnetic interference.
Radio is fragile, weather-dependent, with lag. Radio degradation is a narrative thread that tightens isolation.
The nameless scent — faintly acrid, pungent organic — grows stronger as players approach Shoggoth territory.
timeline: Radio is the only communication. Transportation: dog sledges and Dornier seaplanes (no helicopters). Navigation: compass, sextant, theodolite — maps are incomplete or wrong. Photography: glass plates and early film — evidence is hard to capture and easy to doubt. The expedition is funded, university-backed, but small and not widely publicized.
technology: Radio — shortwave voice and CW (Morse). Byrd broadcast live from Antarctica in 1928; by 1933 the tech is real but unreliable. Ionospheric disruption near magnetic pole, cold cracks components, blizzards destroy antennas, regular blackout windows. Relay chain to Arkham via wire services. Players can hear civilization — until they can't. That's worse than never having it.
Dog sledges — primary surface transport. Dogs are alive, have personalities, react to danger before humans do. Instinct = early warning system. Not equipment — living NPCs.

Dornier seaplanes — open/minimally enclosed cockpit, propeller-driven, ski-mounted. No radar, no blind-flying instruments, no pressurized cabin. Every flight is a gamble. Whiteout = death. Not a taxi.

Navigation — magnetic compass (unreliable near pole), sun compass (useless in overcast), sextant (needs visible horizon), theodolite. Maps are sketch-based, often wrong. Getting lost is the default state.
Photography — hand cameras, individual negatives, deliberate shots. Development happens later. Photos can be blurry, cold-damaged, unconvincing. Evidence is fragile and doubted.

Pabodie's drill — steel head, jointed rods, gasoline motor, collapsible wooden derrick, dynamite for hard strata. Slow, loud, violent. No ground sensors. You don't know what's underneath until you break through.
Fuel — gasoline and kerosene for everything (planes, drill, Primus stoves, heating). Finite and heavy. Every flight, every night in camp burns fuel. Running out = freezing to death.
Medical — no antibiotics (penicillin not until 1940s). Laudanum, morphine, bandages, basic surgery. Frostbite treatment crude. Infected wounds potentially fatal. No medevac.

Cape Evans Hut.origin: Scott's Terra Nova expedition (1910–13), later occupied by the Ross Sea party (1915–17)
Cape Evans Hut.description: Primary shore base. A palimpsest of four expeditions. Contains accumulated material from Discovery, Nimrod, Terra Nova, and Ross Sea party occupations. By the 1930s, a layered archaeological record of Antarctic survival.
discoverableObjects: Richards' inscription — names of three dead men scratched on the wall near a bunk. Spencer-Smith, Mackintosh, Hayward. Still legible.
Canvas clothing from 'Joyce's Famous Tailoring Shop' — garments cut from a Terra Nova tent. Bizarre handmade coats and trousers.
'Hut Point Mixture' — a tin of homemade tobacco substitute (sawdust, tea, coffee, dried herbs). Labeled in Wild's handwriting. Smells terrible.
Journals and diaries — fragments from Mackintosh, Joyce, Richards. Primary accounts of the depot-laying ordeal.
Conflicting orders from Shackleton — documents showing contradictory instructions that got people killed.
The unsent cable — Shackleton wrote it, never sent it. Would have prevented the first season's disastrous depot run.
Scientific instruments — remnants from the Ross Sea party's research team (physics, meteorology, biology).
Dog harnesses and equipment — sized for animals that didn't survive. Six dogs lived through winter; most died on the march.
Broken Primus stoves — from multiple expeditions. Some functional, some not. A working one could be critical.
Scott's supplies (1910–13) — scavenged by the Ross Sea party. Some remain.
Improvised sledging equipment — patched from parts of multiple expeditions, marks of desperate repair.

Hut Point.description: Edge of the Barrier. Natural starting point for southern journeys. The Ross Sea party was stranded here March–June 1915, living on seal meat and blubber. The oldest European structure in Antarctica.
discoverableObjects: Minimal supplies — evidence of desperate habitation.
Cached sledging gear from multiple expeditions.
Seal bones and blubber residue from the 1915 stranding.

Minna Bluff Depot.description: First major depot site on the route south. Laid by the Ross Sea party during both seasons. The first season's push to this point killed all ten dogs.
discoverableObjects: Depot stores or their remains — flags, markers, cached fuel and rations.
Possible dog remains from the first season's march.

80S Depot.description: Second depot site. Reaching this point during the first season cost all ten sled dogs their lives.
discoverableObjects: Cached supplies — 15+ years old by the 1930s. Pemmican and hardtack survive, but the question is whether players trust them.

Mount Hope Depot.description: Final depot. The furthest point the Ross Sea party reached. Also the location closest to the mountains beyond. 198 continuous days of sledging to lay these depots — the longest march in Antarctic history.
discoverableObjects: Ernest Wild's letter to his brother Frank — undelivered, uncollected. Written assuming Frank was crossing from the Weddell Sea. Frank never came. A letter to someone who was never going to arrive.
Depot supplies — the southernmost cache. Never used. The depots were never needed.

historicContext.rossSea Party: The Ross Sea party (1914–17) laid depots across the Ice Barrier for Shackleton's transcontinental crossing. The crossing never happened — the Endurance was crushed. Three men died completing a mission that was for nothing. Their ship, the Aurora, was torn from its moorings and drifted for nine months. After the rescue, the Aurora vanished on a coal run — never seen again.
historicContext.expeditionLayers: By the 1930s, McMurdo Sound sites carry remains from four expeditions: Discovery (1901–04), Nimrod (1907–09), Terra Nova (1910–13), and the Ross Sea party (1914–17). Every hut, cache, and inscription is a layer in this palimpsest.
historicContext.thematicResonance: The Ross Sea party's story mirrors the game's core themes: missions pursued past the point of reason, institutional betrayal, cosmic futility, and whether human endurance means anything. Richards said yes — 'it was something the human spirit had accomplished.' Lovecraft's cosmos says no. The players decide.
historicContext.theAurora: The SY Aurora was torn from its moorings at Cape Evans in May 1915, drifted for nine months, was repaired, rescued the survivors, then vanished forever on a coal run in 1917. A ship that survived Antarctica only to be swallowed by the ordinary ocean. The Keeper can deploy this as atmosphere — radio ghosts, cautionary tales, the precedent that ships don't always come back.
historicContext.heroicAgeLegacy: Scott died 1912 (20 years ago). Shackleton died 1922 (11 years ago). Amundsen disappeared 1928 (5 years ago). These are recent legends, not distant history. The Antarctic community is small and haunted by its losses. Going to Antarctica is known to kill people.
historicContext.whalingIndustry: Antarctic whaling at its peak. Factory ships in the Ross Sea. Whalers are the most experienced Antarctic navigators — they know ice, weather, coastlines. Also a source of rumors, superstitions, and observations scientists dismiss.
historicContext.roerichConnection: Nicholas Roerich — Russian painter famous for luminous, mystical Himalayan landscapes. Also a genuine mystic who led a 1920s expedition searching for Shambhala. Lovecraft compares the Antarctic mountains to Roerich's paintings. The implication: someone thinks there's something hidden in those mountains.
historicContext.poeConnection: Poe's 1838 Narrative of Arthur Gordon Pym ends in Antarctica — milky white curtain, the cry 'Tekeli-li.' Lovecraft's Shoggoths echo this. Within the story's logic, Poe wasn't writing fiction but recording garbled real encounters. A well-read character recognizes this with horror.
historicContext.rareBooksAndNecronomicon: Rare texts circulate through university libraries and private collectors. No internet — physical travel to the holding library required. Danforth has read the Necronomicon at Miskatonic's library, one of very few copies. This is dangerous, unusual, and marks him.
